title: Hooks, Configuration and Aliases
learningObjectives: Configure Git at global, local, and system scopes and understand the precedence order
Write client-side hooks (pre-commit, commit-msg) to enforce project standards
Understand .gitignore pattern syntax and debug ignored files
Use git config --list and --show-origin to audit current configuration

theory.content:
Git Configuration Scopes

Git configuration is layered across three scopes. Local overrides global, which overrides system.

  • System — /etc/gitconfig — applies to every user on the machine
  • Global — ~/.gitconfig or ~/.config/git/config — applies to all repos for your user
  • Local — .git/config inside a repo — applies only to that repository
# Show all config values and the file each one comes from
git config --list --show-origin

# Set your identity globally (stored in ~/.gitconfig)
git config --global user.name "Alice Smith"
git config --global user.email "alice@example.com"

# Set VS Code as the editor (--wait makes Git wait for the window to close)
git config --global core.editor "code --wait"

# Normalise line endings on Windows
git config --global core.autocrlf true

# Default branch name for new repos
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# Always rebase instead of merge on git pull
git config --global pull.rebase true

# Read a single value
git config --global user.email

Aliases

Aliases let you create short forms of long or frequently used Git commands. They are stored under the [alias] section of your ~/.gitconfig.

# Create an alias via the command line
git config --global alias.st status
git st  # now runs git status

# Pretty graph log
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --decorate --all"

# Unstage a file
git config --global alias.unstage "reset HEAD --"

# Show the last commit
git config --global alias.last "log -1 HEAD"

# List all aliases
git config --global alias.aliases "config --get-regexp alias"

In your ~/.gitconfig the same aliases look like this:

[alias]
    st      = status
    lg      = log --oneline --graph --decorate --all
    unstage = reset HEAD --
    last    = log -1 HEAD
    aliases = config --get-regexp alias

Git Hooks

Hooks are executable shell scripts stored in .git/hooks/, named after the lifecycle event they respond to. Git runs them automatically at the corresponding moment.

Important: hooks live in .git/ and are not committed to the repository. To share hooks with a team, use a tool such as Husky (Node.js) or the pre-commit framework (Python), which install hooks from a config file that is committed.

Make scripts executable: chmod +x .git/hooks/commit-msg

Client-side hooks:

  • pre-commit — runs before the commit message is requested; use for linting, formatting, fast tests
  • prepare-commit-msg — runs after the default message is created but before the editor opens; can auto-populate the message
  • commit-msg — receives the path to the file containing the commit message; use for format validation
  • post-commit — runs after the commit is created; useful for notifications or logging
  • pre-push — runs before pushing; use for slower test suites

Server-side hooks (run on the remote): pre-receive, update, post-receive.

Hook Example: Enforcing Conventional Commits

The Conventional Commits specification requires messages like feat(auth): add OAuth2 login. The following commit-msg hook enforces this pattern:

#!/bin/sh
# .git/hooks/commit-msg
COMMIT_MSG=$(cat "$1")
PATTERN="^(feat|fix|docs|chore|refactor|test|style)(\(.+\))?: .+"
if ! echo "$COMMIT_MSG" | grep -qE "$PATTERN"; then
  echo "ERROR: Commit message does not follow Conventional Commits format."
  echo "Expected: type(scope): description"
  echo "Example:  feat(auth): add OAuth2 login"
  exit 1
fi
exit 0

The hook receives the path to the commit message file as $1. Exiting with a non-zero code aborts the commit.

.gitignore Patterns

Git uses pattern matching in .gitignore to decide which files to exclude from tracking:

  • *.log — any file ending in .log, in any directory
  • build/ — the directory named build (and everything in it)
  • !important.log — negate: do not ignore this specific file
  • **/temp — any file or directory named temp, anywhere in the tree
  • doc/*.txt — .txt files only in the doc/ directory (not deeper)
# Global .gitignore for personal/IDE files
git config --global core.excludesFile ~/.gitignore_global

# Debug why a file is being ignored
git check-ignore -v path/to/file
